provenance.source_corpus: Response of thicket swamp species to soil moisture levels: Implications for restoration

source_spans.text: We tested the effect of soil moisture on wetland shrub growth through three watering treatments: Control, Moist, and Wet.
These soil moisture differences produced a measurable effect on plant growth for six of the nine species tested.
The final aboveground biomass response to the soil moisture treatments was similar to stem growth, with four of seven shrubs and both goldenrods being affected
The response of belowground biomass to soil moisture regime was less significant than for the aboveground response
A significant difference in root-to-shoot ratio (R:S) was found for eight of the species
New growth was compared to mean stomatal conductivity to determine an estimated water use efficiency (WUEe)

Aboveground biomass of Rosa palustris exhibited the ladder-like increase in response to increased soil moisture, with Wet treatment mean biomass nearly twice the aboveground biomass of the Control treatment
There was a ladder-like response in stem growth to soil moisture for Spiraea alba, with the Wet treatment resulting in nearly twice as much stem growth as the Control
Only the upland goldenrod, Solidago altissima, demonstrated the expected increase in WUEe with decreasing soil moisture
Solidago uliginosa grew more in the Moist treatment
Sambucus nigra aboveground biomass was lowest in the Wet soil moisture treatment
Salix interior and Spiraea alba aboveground biomass was strongly restricted in the drier soils
Changing soil moisture did not have an effect on aboveground biomass for either Cornus sericea or Viburnum lentago
Changing soil moisture did not have an effect on aboveground biomass for either Cornus sericea or Viburnum lentago
Changing soil moisture did not have an effect on aboveground biomass for either Cornus sericea or Viburnum lentago
